Task: Deserialize the same JSON field into two different struct fields

I'm trying to deserialize a JSON object, and use the value from one of its fields for two different fields in the Rust struct. I've seen similar questions to this, but all of them were trying to do something more complicated than I am.

My input JSON looks like this:

{
    "name": "Henry"
    "ac": 16,
    "hp": 54
}

And I want to deserialize it into a struct like this:

struct Character {
    name: String,
    ac: u8,
    current: u16,
    max: u16,
    temp: u16
}

Having the current and max HP as separate variables is important for the program, but they should both start at the same value: the HP field from the JSON. The temp field should be 0 when the struct is initialized, so I can easily skip that. This is what I've tried:

#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct Character {
    name: String,
    ac: u8,
    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
    current: u16,
    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
    max: u16,
    #[serde(skip)]
    temp: u16
}

And this I get this error:

warning: unreachable pattern
  --> src/main.rs:10:22
   |
 8 |    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
   |                     ---- matches all the relevant values
 9 |    current: u16,
10 |    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
   |                     ^^^^ no value can reach this
   |

thread 'main' (32) panicked at src/main.rs:18:55:
called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Error("missing field `hp`", line: 1, column: 39)

Everything I can find points to the only solutions being:

  1. Use a helper struct (I'm currently doing this and want to get rid of it to simplify the code)

  2. Implement Deserialize manually (This is above my understanding, and even if I could do it it would be more complicated than my existing code.)

I don't need to do anything fancy with the values, I just want to use the same JSON field as the value for two struct fields. Is this simply impossible with Serde, or am I missing something?

Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

Task: Design dilemma with enum_dispatch in Rust

I'm designing my system which consists of three parts, abstract layer (al) where traits and generic data structures are defined; implementation layer (il) where different structures implement traits; and business layer (bl) where the system runs with given configurations.

Now, the il depends the al because of traits. Meanwhile, the bl depends on both al and il because it uses the public interfaces and specific structures. Now there is a cycle upsetting me.

In the al, there's a trait named Rest which defines lots of network service and it's annotated with #[enum_dispatch]. In the il, both Foo and Boo implement Rest. In the bl, I intend to use enum_dispatch to achieve static dispatch as below:

#[enum_dispatch(Rest)]
pub enum Item {
    Foo,
    Boo
}

If it worked, I'm allowed to directly use methods within the trait on different variants as like dynamic dispatch but with much better performance. However, enum_dispatch requires the annotated trait and enum must be defined within the same crate. If so, I have to integrate the al and the bl, violating my purpose to separate logic. And even if I integrated them to one crate, which we still call al, it's still very tricky because now the al and the il depend on each other, there'll be multiple different versions of crate al in the dependency graph, making it a circle.

Is there any solution that I can still keep a separate hierarchy without integrating the three crates into one? It's almost impossible to manually implement Rest for the enum either because this trait contains almost 20 methods.

    "    let method_name = args",
    "        .method_name",

    "    let input: DeriveInput = parse_macro_input!(input as DeriveInput);",
    "    let generics = &input.generics;",
    "    let (impl_generics, ty_generics, where_clause) = generics.split_for_impl();",

    "    let body = match input.data {",

    "            // Convert the Type to a string representation using ToTokens",

    "                ///",
    "                /// This method is safe, because the macro guarantees that the enum is repr(#typ_string).",

    "}",

    "struct Args {",
    "    typ: Type,",
    "    method_name: Option<Ident>,",
    "}",
    "",
    "impl Parse for Args {",
    "    fn parse(input: ParseStream<'_>) -> syn::Result<Self> {",
    "        let typ: Type = input.parse()?;",
    "",
    "        if input.is_empty() {",
    "            return Ok(Self {",
    "                typ,",
    "                method_name: None,",
    "            });",
    "        };",
    "",
    "        let _: Comma = input.parse()?;",
    "        let method_name = input.parse()?;",
    "",
    "        if !input.is_empty() {",

    "            typ,",
    "            method_name,",
    "        })",
    "    }",
    "}",

Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

prompt:
Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

    "        return false;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    // Handle Option<T> generically\n",
    "    if value_mut\n",
    "        .type_id()\n",
    "        .as_str()\n",
    "        .starts_with(*\"core::option::Option<\")\n",
    "    {\n",
    "        option_propery_generic(ui, title, value_mut, interactable);\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    false\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "fn option_propery_generic(\n",
    "    ui: &mut egui::Ui,\n",
    "    title: &str,\n",
    "    value_any: &mut dyn Any,\n",
    "    interactable: bool,\n",
    ") {\n",
    "    // For Option<T>, use a downcast that matches any Option<T>\n",
    "    // Since we can’t deduce T from `dyn Any`, we use a trick: same as below, we define helper trait.\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "// Better approach: define generic helper traits\n",
    "pub trait AsOption {\n",
    "    fn as_option_mut(&mut self) -> &mut dyn Any;\n",
    "    fn is_option() -> bool;\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "impl<T> AsOption for Option<T> {\n",
    "    fn as_option_mut(&mut self) -> &mut dyn Any {\n",
    "        self\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    fn is_option() -> bool {\n",
    "        true\n",
    "    }\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "pub trait AsVec {\n",
    "    fn as_vec_mut(&mut self) -> &mut dyn Any;\n",
    "    fn is_vec() -> bool;\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "impl<T> AsVec for Vec<T> {\n",
    "    fn as_vec_mut(&mut self) -> &mut dyn Any {\n",
    "        self\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    fn is_vec() -> bool {\n",
    "        true\n",
    "    }\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "// estevão style trait for generic downcasting of Option/Vec\n",
    "pub trait DowncastAny {\n",
    "    fn downcast_mut_vec<T: Any>(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Vec<T>>;\n",
    "    fn downcast_mut_option<T: Any>(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Option<T>>;\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "impl DowncastAny for dyn Any {\n",
    "    fn downcast_mut_vec<T: Any>(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Vec<T>> {\n",
    "        self.downcast_mut::<&mut Vec<T>>()\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    fn downcast_mut_option<T: Any>(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Option<T>> {\n",
    "        self.downcast_mut::<&mut Option<T>>()\n",
    "    }\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "// Main function with generic TableRow\n",
    "pub fn downcast_property(\n",
    "    ui: &mut egui::Ui,\n",
    "    title: &str,\n",
    "    value_mut: &mut dyn Any,\n",
    "    interactable: bool,\n",
    ") {\n",
    "    // 1. Try Option<T> (generic for any T: 'static)\n",
    "    // We iterate over known T by macros (see below). For true generic, use a _trait-based approach._\n",
    "\n",
    "    // Better idea: use procedural macros — but for demo, do manual dispatch via helper function\n",
    "    // that returns bool indicating success.\n",
    "\n",
    "    let known = downcast_known(ui, title, value_mut, interactable);\n",
    "    if known {\n",
    "        return;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    // Now try generic Option/Vec\n",
    "    if let Some(v) = value_mut.downcast_mut::<&mut dyn AsVec>() {\n",
    "        // Only works if AsVec implemented on concrete Vec<T>\n",
    "        unimplemented!(\"Generic Vec<T> only possible if we match T at runtime\");\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    if let Some(o) = value_mut.downcast_mut::<&mut dyn AsOption>() {\n",
    "        option_propery_generic(ui, title, o.as_option_mut(), interactable);\n",
    "        return;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    ui.label(egui::RichText::new(format!(\"Field {}: unknown type\", title)).color(egui::Color32::LIGHT_RED));\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "// Better: use macro to generate cloneable match arms for Vec/Option types\n",
    "macro_rules! match_option_type {\n",
    "    ($ui:expr, $title:expr, $value_mut:expr, $interactable:expr, $known:expr, $( $ty:ty ),* ) => {\n",
    "        $(\n",
    "            if !$known.load(std::sync::atomic::Ordering::Relaxed) {\n",
    "                if let Some(opt) = $value_mut.downcast_mut::<&mut Option<$ty>>() {\n",
    "                    option_propery($ui, $title, opt, $interactable);\n",
    "                    $known.store(true, std::sync::atomic::Ordering::Relaxed);\n",
    "                }\n",
    "            }\n",
    "        )*\n",
    "    }\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "macro_rules! match_vec_type {\n",
    "    ($ui:expr, $title:expr, $value_mut:expr, $interactable:expr, $known:expr, $( $ty:ty ),* ) => {\n",
    "        $(\n",
    "            if !$known.load(std::sync::atomic::Ordering::Relaxed) {\n",
    "                if let Some(vec) = $value_mut.downcast_mut::<&mut Vec<$ty>>() {\n",
    "                    list_property($ui, $title, vec, $interactable);\n",
    "                    $known.store(true, std::sync::atomic::Ordering::Relaxed);\n",
    "                }\n",
    "            }\n",
    "        )*;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "fn downcast_known(\n",
    "    ui: &mut egui::Ui,\n",
    "    title: &str,\n",
    "    value_mut: &mut dyn Any,\n",
    "    interactable: bool,\n",
    ") -> bool {\n",
    "    use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, Ordering};\n",
    "    let mut known = AtomicBool::new(false);\n",
    "\n",
    "    // Strings\n",
    "    if let Some(s) = value_mut.downcast_mut::&mut String() {\n",
    "        string_property(ui, title, s, interactable);\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    // Scalars\n",
    "    if let Some(v) = value_mut.downcast_mut::&mut i32() {\n",
    "        i_property(ui, title, v, interactable);\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    if let Some(v) = value_mut.downcast_mut::&mut f32() {\n",
    "        f_property(ui, title, v, interactable);\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    if let Some(v) = value_mut.downcast_mut::&mut usize() {\n",
    "        i_property(ui, title, v, interactable);\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "    if let Some(v) = value_mut.downcast_mut::&mut bool() {\n",
    "        bool_property(ui, title, v, interactable);\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    // Vecs\n",
    "    match_vec_type!(\n",
    "        ui,\n",
    "        title,\n",
    "        value_mut,\n",
    "        interactable,\n",
    "        known,\n",
    "        String,\n",
    "        i32,\n",
    "        f32,\n",
    "        bool,\n",
    "        DataType,\n",
    "        ValueType,\n",
    "        NamedTypeValue,\n",
    "        ArgumentNamedValue\n",
    "    );\n",
    "    if known.load(Ordering::Relaxed) {\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    // Options\n",
    "    match_option_type!(\n",
    "        ui,\n",
    "        title,\n",
    "        value_mut,\n",
    "        interactable,\n",
    "        known,\n",
    "        String,\n",
    "        i32,\n",
    "        f32,\n",
    "        usize,\n",
    "        bool,\n",
    "        DataType,\n",
    "        ValueType,\n",
    "        NamedTypeValue,\n",
    "        ArgumentNamedValue\n",
    "    );\n",
    "    if known.load(Ordering::Relaxed) {\n",
    "        return true;\n",
    "    }\n",
    "\n",
    "    false\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "pub fn list_property<T>(ui: &mut egui::Ui, title: &str, value_mut: &mut Vec<T>, interactable: bool)\n",
    "where\n",
    "    T: Default + Any + 'static,\n",
    "{\n",
    "    egui::CollapsingHeader::new(title)\n",
    "        .default_open(true)\n",
    "        .enabled(interactable)\n",
    "        .show(ui, |ui| {\n",
    "            ui.horizontal_top(|ui| {\n",
    "                if ui.button(\"➕ Add\").clicked() {\n",
    "                    value_mut.push(T::default());\n",
    "                }\n",
    "            });\n",
    "\n",
    "            let mut i = 0;\n",
    "            while i < value_mut.len() {\n",
    "                ui.horizontal(|ui| {\n",
    "                    downcast_property(ui, \"\", &mut value_mut[i], interactable);\n",
    "                    if ui.button(\"❌\").on_hover_text(\"Remove\").clicked() {\n",
    "                        value_mut.remove(i);\n",
    "                    } else {\n",
    "                        i += 1;\n",
    "                    }\n",
    "                });\n",
    "            }\n",
    "        });\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "pub fn option_propery<T>(ui: &mut egui::Ui, title: &str, value_mut: &mut Option<T>, interactable: bool)\n",
    "where\n",
    "    T: Default + Any + 'static,\n",
    "{\n",
    "    let mut is_none = value_mut.is_none();\n",
    "\n",
    "    ui.horizontal(|ui| {\n",
    "        ui.label(title);\n",
    "        ui.add_space(4.0);\n",
    "        bool_property(ui, \"None\", &mut is_none, interactable);\n",
    "        ui.add_space(2.0);\n",
    "\n",
    "        if is_none {\n",
    "            *value_mut = None;\n",
    "        } else {\n",
    "            if value_mut.is_none() {\n",
    "                *value_mut = Some(T::default());\n",
    "            }\n",
    "            if let Some(inner) = value_mut.as_mut() {\n",
    "                downcast_property(ui, \"\", inner, interactable);\n",
    "            }\n",
    "        }\n",
    "    });\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "// Sample types\n",
    "pub struct DataType;\n",
    "pub struct ValueType;\n",
    "pub struct NamedTypeValue;\n",
    "pub struct ArgumentNamedValue;\n",
    "\n",
    "pub fn string_property(_ui: &mut egui::Ui, _title: &str, _value: &mut String, _interactable: bool) {}\n",
    "pub fn i_property(_ui: &mut egui::Ui, _title: &str, _value: &mut i32, _interactable: bool) {}\n",
    "pub fn f_property(_ui: &mut egui::Ui, _title: &str, _value: &mut f32, _interactable: bool) {}\n",
    "pub fn bool_property(_ui: &mut egui::Ui, _title: &str, _value: &mut bool, _interactable: bool) {}\n",
    "\n",
    "// eframe boilerplate\n",
    "struct App;\n",
    "\n",
    "impl eframe::App for App {\n",
    "    fn update(&mut self, ctx: &egui::Context, _frame: &mut eframe::Frame) {\n",
    "        egui::CentralPanel::default().show(ctx, |ui| {\n",
    "            ui.heading(\"Downcaster Demo\");\n",
    "            ui.horizontal(|ui| {\n",
    "                ui.label(\"This is a demo showing how to downcast Option<_> and Vec<_>\");\n",
    "            });\n",
    "        });\n",
    "    }\n",
    "}\n",
    "\n",
    "fn main() {\n",
    "    let native_options = eframe::NativeOptions::default();\n",
    "    eframe::run_native(\n",
    "        \"Downcast Demo\",\n",
    "        native_options,\n",
    "        Box::new(|_cc| Box::new(App)),\n",
    "    )\n",
    "    .unwrap();\n",
    "}",
    "/// End of main.rs"
  ]
}

prompt:
Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

    "        pub unsafe extern \"C\" fn __init() -> i32 {",
    "            #name::init()",
    "        }",
    "    };",
    "",
    "    TokenStream::from(expanded)",
    "}",
    "",
    "// This is a helper trait to avoid requiring unsafe in user code",
    "pub trait Driver {",
    "    fn init() -> i32;",
    "}",
    "",
    "// User code:",
    "#[export_driver]",
    "struct MyDriver;",
    "",
    "impl Driver for MyDriver {",
    "    fn init() -> i32 {",
    "        0  // success code",
    "    }",
    "}",
    "",
    "#[export_name = \"driver_init\"]",
    "pub extern \"C\" fn driver_init() -> i32 {",
    "    MyDriver::init()",
    "}",
    "",
    "fn main() {",
    "    // Normal Rust code",
    "    println!(\"Driver module compiled successfully\");",
    "}",
    "",
    "pub fn init() -> i32 {",
    "    MyDriver::init()",
    "}"
  ]
}

Task: Design dilemma with enum_dispatch in Rust

I'm designing my system which consists of three parts, abstract layer (al) where traits and generic data structures are defined; implementation layer (il) where different structures implement traits; and business layer (bl) where the system runs with given configurations.

Now, the il depends the al because of traits. Meanwhile, the bl depends on both al and il because it uses the public interfaces and specific structures. Now there is a cycle upsetting me.

In the al, there's a trait named Rest which defines lots of network service and it's annotated with #[enum_dispatch]. In the il, both Foo and Boo implement Rest. In the bl, I intend to use enum_dispatch to achieve static dispatch as below:

#[enum_dispatch(Rest)]
pub enum Item {
    Foo,
    Boo
}

If it worked, I'm allowed to directly use methods within the trait on different variants as like dynamic dispatch but with much better performance. However, enum_dispatch requires the annotated trait and enum must be defined within the same crate. If so, I have to integrate the al and the bl, violating my purpose to separate logic. And even if I integrated them to one crate, which we still call al, it's still very tricky because now the al and the il depend on each other, there'll be multiple different versions of crate al in the dependency graph, making it a circle.

Is there any solution that I can still keep a separate hierarchy without integrating the three crates into one? It's almost impossible to manually implement Rest for the enum either because this trait contains almost 20 methods.
